questions.stem: A single particle moves freely in three-dimensional space with no constraints. How many degrees of freedom does it have?
questions.explanation: The number of degrees of freedom is the number of independent coordinates needed to fix the configuration. A free point particle in space is located by its three Cartesian coordinates $(x, y, z)$, with no constraint relating them, so it has $3$ degrees of freedom. The value $6$ would be the count for a rigid body, which also carries orientation; a structureless particle has no orientation to specify.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A system of $N$ particles moves in three dimensions subject to $k$ independent holonomic constraints. How many degrees of freedom does the system have?
questions.explanation: Without constraints, $N$ particles in three dimensions need $3N$ coordinates. Each independent holonomic constraint is an equation $f(\mathbf{r}_1, \dots, \mathbf{r}_N, t) = 0$ that removes exactly one coordinate's worth of freedom. With $k$ such equations, the number of independent generalized coordinates is $3N - k$. The form $3(N - k)$ is wrong because each constraint removes one degree of freedom from the whole system, not three per particle.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Two point masses are joined by a rigid massless rod of fixed length and are otherwise free to move in three-dimensional space. How many degrees of freedom does this system have?
questions.explanation: Two free particles in space have $2 \times 3 = 6$ coordinates. The rigid rod imposes one holonomic constraint, $|\mathbf{r}_1 - \mathbf{r}_2| = \ell$, a single equation that fixes the separation. So the count is $6 - 1 = 5$ degrees of freedom: three to locate the centre of mass and two angles to orient the rod (its length is fixed, so no radial freedom remains). The answer $6$ forgets the constraint entirely.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A rigid body of three or more non-collinear particles moves freely in three-dimensional space. How many degrees of freedom does it have?
questions.explanation: A rigid body needs three coordinates to locate a reference point, such as its centre of mass, plus three independent angles to specify its orientation, giving $6$ degrees of freedom in space. Counting from particles gives the same answer: $N$ particles have $3N$ coordinates, and rigidity fixes enough interparticle distances to impose $3N - 6$ constraints, leaving $3N - (3N - 6) = 6$. The orientation angles are exactly what distinguishes a rigid body from a single particle.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A simple pendulum consists of a bob on a rigid rod of fixed length swinging in a fixed vertical plane about a fixed pivot. How many degrees of freedom does it have?
questions.explanation: The bob is a single particle, so it starts with three coordinates. Confinement to a fixed plane removes one, and the fixed rod length removes another, leaving one. The single angle the rod makes with the vertical fully specifies the configuration, so the pendulum has $1$ degree of freedom. Choosing that angle as the generalized coordinate is what makes the Lagrangian analysis of the pendulum so compact.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A bead is threaded on a fixed rigid circular wire of radius $a$ and slides along it without friction. How many degrees of freedom does the bead have?
questions.explanation: A free particle has three coordinates, but being threaded on a one-dimensional curve fixes two of them: the wire's shape provides two independent equations relating $x$, $y$, and $z$. Only the position along the wire, for instance the arc length or the angle subtended at the centre, is free, so the bead has $1$ degree of freedom. A single generalized coordinate, the angle around the ring, describes its motion completely.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Which of the following constraints is nonholonomic?
options: A bead confined to a rigid wire bent into a helix
A particle forced to stay on the surface of a fixed sphere of radius $a$
Two masses kept a fixed distance apart by a rigid rod
A gas molecule confined to the inside of a closed spherical container, $r \le a$
questions.explanation: A holonomic constraint can be written as an equality $f(q, t) = 0$ that ties coordinates together. The wire, the sphere surface $r = a$, and the rigid rod $|\mathbf{r}_1 - \mathbf{r}_2| = \ell$ are all equalities, hence holonomic. The container wall gives only an inequality, $r \le a$: inside the container the molecule moves freely and the wall acts only at the boundary. An inequality cannot be turned into an equation among the coordinates, so it is nonholonomic.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A vertical disk of radius $a$ rolls without slipping on a horizontal plane, free to change its heading as it rolls. The rolling condition relates the disk's speed to its rate of rotation. This constraint is best described as
options: holonomic, because rolling fixes the contact point
absent, because rolling imposes no constraint at all
nonholonomic, because the velocity relation cannot be integrated to a relation among the coordinates alone
holonomic, because the disk stays on the plane
questions.explanation: Rolling without slipping links the velocity of the contact point to the angular velocity, a relation among the generalized velocities. When the disk can steer, this velocity relation cannot be integrated into an equation involving only the coordinates: the final position and orientation depend on the path taken, not just the endpoints. A constraint on velocities that is not integrable to a coordinate relation is the defining case of a nonholonomic constraint. Staying on the plane is a separate, genuinely holonomic condition, but it is not the rolling constraint being asked about.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A double pendulum swings in a fixed vertical plane: a rod hangs from a fixed pivot, and a second rod hangs from the end of the first. Both rods are rigid and of fixed length. How many degrees of freedom does the system have?
questions.explanation: There are two point masses in a plane, giving $2 \times 2 = 4$ coordinates to start. Each rigid rod imposes one length constraint, removing two coordinates in total, so $4 - 2 = 2$ degrees of freedom remain. The natural generalized coordinates are the two angles each rod makes with the vertical. The double pendulum is the textbook two-degree-of-freedom system and the simplest mechanical system to show chaotic motion.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle is constrained to move on the surface of a fixed sphere of radius $a$ (a spherical pendulum with a rigid rod). How many degrees of freedom does it have, and what is a natural choice of generalized coordinates?
options: $1$ degree of freedom; the polar angle $\theta$ alone
$2$ degrees of freedom; the radius $r$ and one angle
$2$ degrees of freedom; the two angles $(\theta, \phi)$
questions.explanation: In spherical coordinates a free particle uses $(r, \theta, \phi)$. Fixing the particle to the sphere sets $r = a$, one holonomic constraint, leaving $3 - 1 = 2$ degrees of freedom. The radius is no longer free, so the two surviving coordinates are the angles $(\theta, \phi)$. Choosing $r$ as one of the free coordinates is wrong precisely because the constraint freezes it.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: In Lagrangian mechanics, the generalized velocity $\dot{q}_j$ is defined as
options: the total time derivative $dq_j / dt$ of the generalized coordinate
the second time derivative $d^2 q_j / dt^2$
questions.explanation: A generalized velocity is simply the time derivative of the corresponding generalized coordinate, $\dot{q}_j = dq_j/dt$. In the Lagrangian formalism the coordinates $q_j$ and their velocities $\dot{q}_j$ are treated as the independent variables on which $L(q, \dot{q}, t)$ depends. The second time derivative is the generalized acceleration, and the conjugate momentum is a separate quantity defined as $\partial L / \partial \dot{q}_j$.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: For a single particle, the Cartesian position is expressed in plane polar coordinates by $x = r\cos\theta$ and $y = r\sin\theta$. What is the velocity component $\dot{x}$ in terms of $r$, $\theta$, and their time derivatives?
questions.explanation: Differentiate $x = r\cos\theta$ with respect to time, treating both $r$ and $\theta$ as functions of $t$. The product rule gives $\dot{x} = \dot{r}\cos\theta + r\,\frac{d}{dt}(\cos\theta) = \dot{r}\cos\theta - r\dot{\theta}\sin\theta$, since $\frac{d}{dt}\cos\theta = -\dot{\theta}\sin\theta$. Both terms are needed: one from the changing radius, one from the changing angle. The sign error in $+r\dot{\theta}\sin\theta$ comes from forgetting the minus in the derivative of cosine.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle moves in a plane. Using plane polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$, the square of its speed is $v^2 = \dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2$. Which expression equals $v^2$?
questions.explanation: With $\dot{x} = \dot{r}\cos\theta - r\dot{\theta}\sin\theta$ and $\dot{y} = \dot{r}\sin\theta + r\dot{\theta}\cos\theta$, squaring and adding gives $\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2 = \dot{r}^2(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) + r^2\dot{\theta}^2(\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta)$, since the cross terms cancel. The Pythagorean identity reduces this to $v^2 = \dot{r}^2 + r^2\dot{\theta}^2$. The factor $r^2$ on the angular term is essential and is what makes the kinetic energy $T = \tfrac{1}{2}m(\dot{r}^2 + r^2\dot{\theta}^2)$.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: The configuration space of a mechanical system is best described as
options: the abstract space whose points are the allowed sets of generalized coordinates $(q_1, \dots, q_n)$
the space of all positions and momenta together
the space of the generalized velocities $(\dot{q}_1, \dots, \dot{q}_n)$
questions.explanation: Configuration space is the abstract space whose coordinates are the independent generalized coordinates of the system; a single point in it specifies the instantaneous configuration of the whole system. Its dimension equals the number of degrees of freedom. The space that also carries momenta is phase space, of dimension $2n$, used in the Hamiltonian formulation. Configuration space need not resemble ordinary physical space; for a double pendulum it is a torus, not a region of three-dimensional space.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A constraint is called scleronomic when
options: it depends on the velocities and cannot be integrated
it does not contain the time explicitly, so the constraint surface does not move
it is an inequality rather than an equation
it changes with time, like a support that is being raised
questions.explanation: A scleronomic constraint is one with no explicit time dependence: $f(q) = 0$ rather than $f(q, t) = 0$. The constraint surface is fixed in time, as for a bead on a stationary wire. A constraint that does contain time explicitly, such as a wire being raised so that the constraint reads $f(q, t) = 0$, is called rheonomic. The velocity-dependence and inequality descriptions refer instead to the holonomic versus nonholonomic distinction, a separate classification.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A free rigid rod (a thin rod, not a point) moves in a fixed two-dimensional plane. How many degrees of freedom does it have?
questions.explanation: A rigid body confined to a plane needs two coordinates to place a reference point, such as the centre of the rod, plus one angle to give its orientation in the plane, for a total of $3$ degrees of freedom. Counting from endpoints agrees: two point ends give $4$ coordinates in the plane, and the fixed rod length removes one, leaving $4 - 1 = 3$. A planar rigid body always has three degrees of freedom, two translational and one rotational.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Three particles move freely in three-dimensional space, joined by three rigid rods into a rigid triangle. How many degrees of freedom does the assembly have?
questions.explanation: Three free particles in space have $3 \times 3 = 9$ coordinates. Three rigid rods fix the three pairwise distances, giving three independent holonomic constraints, so $9 - 3 = 6$ degrees of freedom remain. This matches the general result that any rigid body in space has six degrees of freedom: three for the position of a reference point and three for orientation. A rigid triangle of three masses is the smallest such rigid body.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Why are well-chosen generalized coordinates advantageous over Cartesian coordinates when a system has holonomic constraints?
options: Because Cartesian coordinates cannot describe curved motion of any kind
Because generalized coordinates make the kinetic energy a constant
Because the forces of constraint always vanish in any coordinate system

Because generalized coordinates can be chosen to automatically satisfy the constraints, reducing the count to the independent degrees of freedom and eliminating the constraint forces from the equations
questions.explanation: If the generalized coordinates are chosen to satisfy the holonomic constraints identically, then only as many coordinates as there are degrees of freedom remain, and the unknown forces of constraint drop out of the Lagrange equations. That is the central practical advantage of the Lagrangian method: one works with the minimal set of independent variables and never has to compute the constraint forces to find the motion. Cartesian coordinates can describe curved motion perfectly well, and the kinetic energy is generally not constant, so the other statements are false.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle slides on the inside of a fixed frictionless cone whose axis is vertical and whose half-angle is fixed. How many degrees of freedom does the particle have?
questions.explanation: A free particle has three coordinates. The cone's surface is two-dimensional, so confinement to it imposes one holonomic constraint (in cylindrical coordinates, $z = \rho\cot\alpha$ with $\alpha$ fixed). That leaves $3 - 1 = 2$ degrees of freedom, naturally taken as the distance up the cone and the azimuthal angle around the axis. A surface always leaves two degrees of freedom, just as a curve leaves one.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A dumbbell of two equal point masses joined by a rigid rod moves in a fixed vertical plane. How many degrees of freedom does it have?
questions.explanation: Two point masses in a plane give $2 \times 2 = 4$ coordinates. The rigid rod fixes their separation, one holonomic constraint, leaving $4 - 1 = 3$ degrees of freedom. These are exactly the three of any rigid body in a plane: two to place the centre of mass and one angle for the orientation of the rod. The dumbbell is a planar rigid body, so three is the expected count.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A system of $N = 4$ particles moves in three-dimensional space subject to $k = 5$ independent holonomic constraints. Find the number of degrees of freedom.
questions.solution: Unconstrained, the particles need $3N = 3 \times 4 = $ {{1}} coordinates. Each independent holonomic constraint removes one, so the number of degrees of freedom is $3N - k = $ {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The unconstrained count is $3 \times 4 = 12$. Subtracting the five constraints gives $12 - 5 = 7$ degrees of freedom. Each holonomic constraint is one equation and removes exactly one coordinate from the independent set.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Classify each constraint as holonomic or nonholonomic. (a) A bead is fixed to a rigid circular wire. (b) A gas molecule is confined inside a closed box, so its coordinates satisfy only inequalities.
questions.solution: A constraint that can be written as an equation $f(q, t) = 0$ among the coordinates is called {{1}}. The bead on the wire is therefore {{2}}, while the molecule confined by the walls of the box, governed by inequalities only, is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Holonomic constraints are equalities relating the coordinates (and possibly time). The bead on a rigid wire satisfies such equations, so it is holonomic. The walls of a box give only inequalities on the molecule's position, which cannot be reduced to coordinate equations, so that constraint is nonholonomic.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Count the degrees of freedom for two standard systems. (a) A simple pendulum: a bob on a rigid rod swinging in a fixed vertical plane. (b) A spherical pendulum: a bob on a rigid rod free to swing in any direction about a fixed pivot.
questions.solution: The simple pendulum has {{1}} degree(s) of freedom, described by a single swing angle. The spherical pendulum keeps the rod length fixed but allows motion over a sphere, giving {{2}} degrees of freedom.
questions.explanation: The planar simple pendulum needs only the angle from the vertical, so it has $1$ degree of freedom. The spherical pendulum fixes only the rod length ($r = \ell$), leaving the two angles $(\theta, \phi)$ on the sphere, so it has $2$ degrees of freedom.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle moves in a plane described by polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$. Express the square of its speed and identify the kinetic energy.
questions.solution: Differentiating $x = r\cos\theta$ and $y = r\sin\theta$ and squaring, the speed satisfies $v^2 = \dot{r}^2 + $ {{1}} (fill the angular term). The kinetic energy of a mass $m$ is then $T = $ {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The cross terms cancel on squaring, leaving $v^2 = \dot{r}^2 + r^2\dot{\theta}^2$, where the angular speed contributes $r^2\dot{\theta}^2$. Multiplying by $\tfrac{1}{2}m$ gives the planar kinetic energy $T = \tfrac{1}{2}m(\dot{r}^2 + r^2\dot{\theta}^2)$, the standard starting point for central-force Lagrangians.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A rigid body made of three or more non-collinear particles moves freely. Count its degrees of freedom in three dimensions and in a plane, and split the three-dimensional count into translational and rotational parts.
questions.solution: In three-dimensional space a rigid body has {{1}} degrees of freedom, of which $3$ are translational and {{2}} are rotational. Confined to a plane, the same body has {{3}} degrees of freedom.
questions.explanation: A rigid body in space has $6$ degrees of freedom: $3$ to locate a reference point plus $3$ orientation angles, so the rotational part is $6 - 3 = 3$. In a plane only one rotation angle survives and one translational direction is lost, leaving $2$ translational and $1$ rotational, that is $3$ in total.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Distinguish configuration space from phase space for a system with $n$ degrees of freedom, and give the dimension of each.
questions.solution: Configuration space has the generalized coordinates as its axes, so its dimension is {{1}} (in terms of $n$). Phase space adds the conjugate momenta, doubling the count to {{2}}. The space whose single point fixes only the system's configuration, without its momenta, is the {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Configuration space is spanned by the $n$ generalized coordinates, so it is $n$-dimensional. Phase space is spanned by the $n$ coordinates together with their $n$ conjugate momenta, giving dimension $2n$. A point of configuration space records only where the system is, not how its momenta are distributed.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics
